questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the 'Isolation' property in ACID transactions?

options.text: Once a transaction commits, its effects survive system failures
Concurrently executing transactions appear to execute one after another, hiding their intermediate state from each other
A transaction either completes entirely or has no effect at all
The database always moves from one valid state to another valid state

questions.explanation: Isolation means concurrent transactions should not see each other's partial effects — the result is equivalent to some serial execution. The other options describe Durability (A), Atomicity (C), and Consistency (D).

options.text: Only conflict-serializable schedules and freedom from cascading aborts
View-serializable schedules but allows cascading aborts
Linearizable reads but not serializable writes

questions.explanation: Strict 2PL holds all exclusive locks until commit/abort, which both ensures conflict-serializability AND prevents cascading aborts (no other transaction can read uncommitted data). However, it does not eliminate deadlocks.

questions.prompt: In the ARIES recovery algorithm, the three phases performed at restart after a crash are, in order:
questions.explanation: ARIES recovery proceeds in three phases: (1) Analysis identifies dirty pages and active transactions from the log; (2) Redo reapplies all logged updates to bring the database to its crash-time state; (3) Undo rolls back transactions that did not commit.

questions.prompt: Phantom reads in concurrent transactions are most directly prevented by which isolation level?
questions.explanation: Phantom reads (rows appearing/disappearing because another transaction inserted/deleted matching rows) are only fully prevented at the Serializable isolation level. Repeatable Read prevents non-repeatable reads on existing rows but does not, by itself, stop phantoms in the SQL standard sense.

questions.prompt: Which type of NoSQL database is most appropriate for storing and querying highly inter-connected data such as social networks, where traversing relationships is the primary access pattern?
questions.explanation: Graph databases store nodes and edges as first-class citizens and are optimized for traversals (e.g., 'friends of friends'). Document and key-value stores treat relationships as secondary; column-family stores are best for very large, sparse, write-heavy tabular data.
